question:

The 5f electrons in actinoids are less effectively shielded and more available for bonding compared to the 4f electrons in lanthanoids.

question: When permanganate ion is heated at 513 K , led to the formation of two manganese based products. The physical properties of the product in which manganese with the higher oxidation state than the other are
explanation:
$\mathrm{KMnO}_{4}$ decomposes on heating at 513 K to give $\mathrm{K}_{2} \mathrm{MnO}_{4}$ and $\mathrm{MnO}_{2}$.
Oxidation state of Mn is higher in $\mathrm{K}_{2} \mathrm{MnO}_{4}$, i.e. + ${\rm{ + 6}}{\rm{.}}$
$\mathrm{Mn}^{+6} \rightarrow[\mathrm{Ar}] 3 \mathrm{~d}^{1} 4 \mathrm{~s}^{0}$
So, $\mathrm{K}_{2} \mathrm{MnO}_{4}$ is paramagnetic and green coloured.

question: One third litre of  ${\text{xM}}{{\text{K}}_2}{\text{C}}{{\text{r}}_2}{{\text{O}}_7}$  is required to completely oxidise 2L of 0.1 M ferrous ammonium sulphate in acid medium. What is  ${\text{x}}$ ?
explanation:
$\mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}^{-2}+6 \mathrm{Fe}^{+2}+14 \mathrm{H}^{+} \rightarrow$ $2 \mathrm{Cr}^{+3}+6 \mathrm{Fe}^{+3}+7 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952073/preptracker/questions/q_106962_explanation_1764952072645.png
$\therefore \dfrac{\mathrm{M}_{1} \mathrm{~V}_{1}}{\mathrm{n}_{1}}=\dfrac{\mathrm{M}_{2} \mathrm{~V}_{2}}{\mathrm{n}_{2}}$
$\mathrm{M}_{1}=\dfrac{\mathrm{M}_{2} \mathrm{~V}_{2} \mathrm{n}_{1}}{\mathrm{n}_{2} \mathrm{~V}_{1}}=\dfrac{0.1 \times 2 \times 1}{6 \times \dfrac{1}{3}}=0.1 \mathrm{M}$.

question:
Out of the 5 different products formed in the following reaction, how many contains O -atom(s)? ____ .
${\mathrm{\mathrm{KMnO}_{4}+\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4} \longrightarrow}}$
explanation:
The chemical reaction is
${\mathrm{2 \mathrm{KMnO}_{4}+5 \mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}+8 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}}}$
$\begin{array}{r}\longrightarrow \mathrm{K}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}+2 \mathrm{MnSO}_{4}+5 \mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4} \\+8 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+5 \mathrm{O}_{2}\end{array}$
Hence, number of products containing O -atom(s) is 5 .

question:
100 mL of 0.1 M ${\rm{KMn}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{4}}}$ solution reacts completely with 50 mL of ${\mathrm{\mathrm{FeSO}_{4}}}$ solution in acidic medium. The mass of ${\mathrm{\mathrm{FeSO}_{4}}}$ in 50 mL of ${\mathrm{\mathrm{FeSO}_{4}}}$ solution is ____ g.
[Molar mass of ${\mathrm{\mathrm{FeSO}_{4}=152 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}}$ ]
explanation:
Chemical reaction is:
$5{\rm{F}}{{\rm{e}}^{2 + {\rm{ }}}}{\rm{ + MnO}}_4^{{\rm{ }} - {\rm{ }}} + 8{{\rm{H}}^{ + {\rm{ }}}} \to $
${\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{\text{M}}{{\text{n}}^{{\text{2 + }}}}{\text{ + 4}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{O + 5F}}{{\text{e}}^{{\text{3 + }}}}}$
${\mathrm{\therefore 5 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~Fe}^{2+} \equiv 1 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~MnO}_{4}^{-}}}$
Now, 1000 mL of ${\mathrm{1 \mathrm{~M} \mathrm{~KMnO}_{4} \equiv 1 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~MnO}_{4}^{-}}}$
100 mL of ${\mathrm{0.1 \mathrm{~M} \mathrm{~KMn} \mathrm{O}_{4}=0.01 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~MnO}_{4}^{-}}}$
${\mathrm{0.01 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~MnO}_{4}^{-} \equiv 0.05 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~Fe}^{2+}}}$
$\begin{aligned}& =0.05 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~FeSO}_{4} \\& =0.05 \times 152 \\& =7.6 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~FeSO}_{4}\end{aligned}$

question:
Read Statement A and Statement B carefully and mark correct option.
Aqueous solution of $\mathrm{K}_{2} \mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}$ is preferred as a primary standard in volumetric analysis over $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}$ aqueous solution.
$\mathrm{K}_{2} \mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}$ has a higher solubility in water than $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}$.
explanation: Aqueous solution of $\mathrm{K}_{2} \mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}$  is preferred as a primary standard in volumetric analysis over $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}$ aqueous solution. $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}$ is hygroscopic while the $\mathrm{K}_{2} \mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}$  is not. Sodium dichromate is more soluble in water than potassium dichromate due to smaller size of sodium ions, as compared to potassium ions. Hence, statement A is true, but statement B is false.

question:
Assertion: The correct order of oxidising power is :
$\mathrm{VO}_{2}^{+} < \mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}^{2-} < \mathrm{MnO}_{4}^{-}$.


Reason: The oxidation state of $\mathrm{Mn}$ is +7 .
explanation:
The oxidation states of the given compounds are
$\begin{aligned}& \mathrm{VO}_{2}^{+} ; \mathrm{x}+2(-2)=+1 \\& \Rightarrow \mathrm{x}=+5 \\& \mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}^{2}: 2 \mathrm{x}+7(-2)=-2 \\& \Rightarrow 2 \mathrm{x}=+12 \\& \Rightarrow \mathrm{x}=+6 \\& \mathrm{MnO}_{4}^{-}: \mathrm{x}+4(-2)=-1 \\& \mathrm{x}=-1+8=+7\end{aligned}$
The correct order of oxidising power is :
$\mathrm{VO}_{2}^{+} < \mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}^{-2} < \mathrm{MnO}_{4}^{-}$
So option (2) is correct.

Assertion is incorrect but Reason is correct.

question:

$\mathop {\mathop {{{\text{K}}_2}{\text{C}}{{\text{r}}_2}{{\text{O}}_7}}\limits_{{\text{ (orange) }}} + 4{{\text{H}}_2}{\text{S}}{{\text{O}}_4}}\limits_{} \to {{\text{K}}_2}{\text{S}}{{\text{O}}_4}{\text{ }}$
$ + \mathop {{\text{C}}{{\text{r}}_2}{{\left( {{\text{S}}{{\text{O}}_4}} \right)}_3}}\limits_{{\text{ (green) }}} + 4{{\text{H}}_2}{\text{O}} + \frac{3}{2}{{\text{O}}_2}$
So option (3) is correct.

$\mathrm{K}_{2} \mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}$ can be found in pure state and can be accurately weighed. It is not hygroscopic in nature. So, $\mathrm{K}_{2} \mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}$ is used as a primary standard solution. Thus, option (3) is correct.

question:
Assertion: Solution of $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CrO}_{4}$ in water is intensely coloured.


Reason: Oxidation state of $\mathrm{Cr}$ in $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CrO}_{4}$ is $+\mathrm{VI}$.
explanation:
$\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CrO}_{4}$ solution in water is yellow in colour, when acidified it changes to orange colour (due to the formation of $\mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}{ }^{2-}$ ions).
$2 \mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4} \rightarrow$
$\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}+\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
Let oxidation state of $\mathrm{Cr}$ in $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ be x.
$\Rightarrow 2(+1)+\mathrm{x}+4(-2)=0 \Rightarrow \mathrm{x}=+6$.
So option (2) is correct.

Thus, a standard solution of $\mathrm{Fe}^{2+}$ ions can be obtained directly by weighing a known amount of the Mohr salt. So option (3) is correct.

Assertion: Aqueous solution of $\mathrm{FeCl}_{3}$ is acidic.

Reason: The hydrated form of $\mathrm{FeCl}_{3}$ consists
of six water molecules, $\mathrm{FeCl}_{3} \cdot 6 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$.
explanation:
$\mathrm{FeCl}_{3}$ in water shows acidic nature because of cationic hydrolysis of $\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}$.
$\mathrm{FeCl}_{3}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \rightarrow \mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{OH})_{3}+3 \mathrm{HCl}$
Given reason is also correct, but it is not the correct explanation of the assertion. So option (2) is correct.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but Reason is correct.

subject: Chemistry

question:
Assertion: $\mathrm{AgBr}$ is used in photography.

Reason: $\mathrm{AgBr}$ undergoes photochemical reaction.
explanation:
$\mathrm{AgBr}$ is the most sensitive silver halide to photoreduction. Hence, it is used as the light sensitive material in photographic films. The unchanged $\mathrm{AgBr}$ is dissolved in hypo solution to cast an image on photographic plate.
$2 \mathrm{AgBr} \xrightarrow{\text { hu }} 2 \mathrm{Ag}+\mathrm{Br}_{2}$.
So option (1) is correct.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but Reason is correct.

subject: Chemistry

question:
Assertion: $\mathrm{KMnO}_{4}$ is stored in dark bottles.

Reason: On heating with alkalies, $\mathrm{KMnO}_{4}$ is converted to manganate.
explanation: Potassium permanganate $\left(\mathrm{KMnO}_{4}\right)$ is stored in opaque containers to prevent photochemical decomposition induced by light, particularly UV radiation, which can degrade the compound. So option (2) is correct.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but Reason is correct.

question:
Pyrolusite is $\mathrm{MnO}_{2}$, and is used to prepare $\mathrm{KMnO}_{4.}$ The Steps are
$\mathrm{MnO}_{2} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{I}} \mathrm{MnO}_{4}^{2-} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{II}} \mathrm{MnO}_{4}^{-}$
Steps I and II are respectively:
explanation:
$\mathrm{MnO}_{2}$ fused with $\mathrm{KOH}$ in presence of air or oxidising agents like $\mathrm{KNO}_{3}, \mathrm{KClO}_{3}$ etc.
$2 \mathrm{MnO}_{2}+4 \mathrm{KOH}+\mathrm{O}_{2} \xrightarrow{\text { fuse }} 2 \mathrm{~K}_{2} \mathrm{MnO}_{4}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
${\text{MnO}}_4^{2 - }\xrightarrow{{{\text{ electrolytic oxidation }}}}{\text{MnO}}_4^ - + {{\text{e}}^ - }$

options.text: Fuse with $\mathrm{KOH} /$ air and electrolytic oxidation.

question: Why is $\mathrm{HCl}$ not used to make the medium acidic in oxidation reactions of $\mathrm{KMnO}_{4}$ in acidic medium?
explanation: $\mathrm{KMnO}_{4}$ oxidises $\mathrm{HCl}$ to $\mathrm{Cl}_{2}$. So, $\mathrm{HCl}$ is not used for acid medium.

options.text: Both $\mathrm{HCl}$ and $\mathrm{KMnO}_{4}$ act as oxidising agents.
$\mathrm{KMnO}_{4}$ oxidises $\mathrm{HCl}$ into $\mathrm{Cl}_{2}$ which is also an oxidising agent.
$\mathrm{KMnO}_{4}$ is a weaker oxidising agent than $\mathrm{HCl}$.
$\mathrm{KMnO}_{4}$ acts as a reducing agent in the presence of $\mathrm{HCl}$.

question: $4 \mathrm{~K}_{2} \mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7} \xrightarrow{\text { heat }} 4 \mathrm{~K}_{2} \mathrm{CrO}_{4}+3 \mathrm{O}_{2}+\mathrm{X}$. In the above reaction ' $\mathrm{X}$ ' is
explanation: Potassium dichromate, on heating gives $\mathrm{K}_{2} \mathrm{CrO}_{4}$, oxygen and chromic oxide $\left(\mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}\right)$. $4 \mathrm{~K}_{2} \mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7} \stackrel{\Delta}{\longrightarrow} 4 \mathrm{~K}_{2} \mathrm{CrO}_{4}+2 \mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}+3 \mathrm{O}_{2}$
subject: Chemistry

question: Acidified potassium dichromate is treated with hydrogen sulphide. In the reaction, the oxidation number of chromium
explanation:
Decreases from +6 to +3 .
$\mathrm{K}_{2} \mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}+4 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{~S} \rightarrow$
$\mathrm{K}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}+\mathrm{Cr}_{2}\left(\mathrm{SO}_{4}\right)_{3}+7 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+3 \mathrm{~S}$
subject: Chemistry

question:
In acidic medium potassium dichromate acts as an oxidant according to the equation,
$\mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}^{2-}+14 \mathrm{H}^{+}+6 \mathrm{e}^{-} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{Cr}^{3+}+7 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$. What is the equivalent weight of $\mathrm{K}_{2} \mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}$ ? (mol.
Wt. $=$ M)
explanation:
No. of electrons involved are 6.
So, equivalent weight ${\text{ = }}\frac{{\text{M}}}{{\text{6}}}$

question: $\mathrm{CrO}_{3}$ dissolves in aqueous $\mathrm{NaOH}$ to give
explanation: $\mathrm{CrO}_{3}+2 \mathrm{NaOH} \rightarrow \underset{\text { Yellow solution }}{\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CrO}_{4}}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
subject: Chemistry

question: Which oxide of manganese is amphoteric.?
explanation: $\mathrm{MnO}_{2}$ is amphoteric oxide.
subject: Chemistry

question: Potassium permanganate acts as an oxidant in neutral, alkaline as well as in acidic media. The final products obtained from it in the three conditions are, respectively
explanation:
In neutral medium:
$2 \mathrm{KMnO}_{4}+3 \mathrm{MnSO}_{4}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \rightarrow$
$\mathrm{K}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}+5 \mathrm{MnO}_{2}$
In alkaline medium:
$2 \mathrm{KMnO}_{4}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{MnO}_{2}+2 \mathrm{KOH}+3[\mathrm{O}]$
In acidic medium:
$2 \mathrm{KMnO}_{4}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4} \rightarrow$
$\mathrm{K}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}+2 \stackrel{(+2)}{\mathrm{MnSO}_{4}}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+5[\mathrm{O}]$
subject: Chemistry

question: Which of the following is a constituent of lithopone?
explanation: Lithopone is $\mathrm{BaSO}_{4} \cdot \mathrm{ZnS}$
subject: Chemistry

question: $\mathrm{Mn}^{2+}$ can be converted into $\mathrm{Mn}^{7+}$ by reacting with
explanation: Strong oxidising agents such as $\mathrm{PbO}_{2}$ or sodium bismuthate $\left(\mathrm{NaBiO}_{3}\right)$ oxidise $\mathrm{Mn}^{2+}$ to $\mathrm{MnO}_{4}^{-}$or $\mathrm{Mn}^{7+}$.
subject: Chemistry

question: Which of the following can react with $\mathrm{K}_{2} \mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}$ ?
explanation:
$\mathrm{K}_{2} \mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}$ cannot oxidise $\mathrm{SO}_{4}^{-2}, \mathrm{CO}_{3}^{-2}, \mathrm{NO}_{3}^{-}$as these are already in highest oxidised forms.
$\mathrm{SO}_{3}^{-2}$ reacts with $\mathrm{K}_{2} \mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}$ as follows :
$\begin{aligned}& \mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}^{2-}(\mathrm{aq})+3 \mathrm{SO}_{3}^{-2}(\mathrm{aq})+8 \mathrm{H}^{+}(\mathrm{aq}) \\& \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{Cr}^{3+}(\mathrm{aq})+3 \mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2-}(\mathrm{aq})+4 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})\end{aligned}$
subject: Chemistry

question:
Assertion: Solution of $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CrO}_{4}$ in water is intensely coloured.


Reason: Oxidation state of $\mathrm{Cr}$ in $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CrO}_{4}$ is $(+\mathrm{VI})$.
explanation:
In ${\text{N}}{{\text{a}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{Cr}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{4}}}{\text{,}}$ is in +6 oxidation state with $\mathrm{d}^{\circ}$ configuration. The $\mathrm{CrO}_{4}^{2-}$ ions in aqueous solution give yellow colour which is not due to $\mathrm{d}-\mathrm{d}$ transition but due to charge transfer, i.e., momentary transfer of charge from oxygen atom to metal atom.
Hence, both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of Assertion.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Both Assertion and Reason is incorrect.

question: The oxidation state of chromium in the final product formed by the reaction between $\mathrm{KI}$ and acidified potassium dichromate solution is
explanation:
$\mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}^{2-}+14 \mathrm{H}^{+}+6 \mathrm{I}^{-} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{Cr}^{3+}+7 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+3 \mathrm{I}_{2}$
Thus, oxidation state of $\mathrm{Cr}$ in product is +3 .
subject: Chemistry

explanation:

question:
Which of the following statements are INCORRECT?
I. All the transition metals except scandium form MO oxides which are ionic.
II. The highest oxidation number corresponding to the group number in transition metal oxides is attained in $\mathrm{Sc}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ to $\mathrm{Mn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}$.
III. Basic character increases from $\mathrm{V}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ to $\mathrm{V}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ to $\mathrm{V}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5}$.
IV. $\mathrm{V}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ dissolves in acids to give $\mathrm{VO}_{4}^{3-}$ salts.
V. $\mathrm{CrO}$ is basic but $\mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ is amphoteric.
Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
explanation:
Option $\mathrm{III} \rightarrow \stackrel{+3}{\mathrm{~V}_{2}} \mathrm{O}_{3} \rightarrow \stackrel{+4}{\mathrm{~V}_{2}} \mathrm{O}_{4} \rightarrow \stackrel{+5}{\mathrm{~V}_{2}} \mathrm{O}_{5}$ as oxidation state increases, acidic nature increases.
Option $\mathrm{IV} \rightarrow \mathrm{V}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5}$ dissolves in acid to give $\mathrm{VO}_{4}^{-3}$ salts $\mathrm{V}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$.
subject: Chemistry

question: Aqueous solution of a salt (A) forms a dense white precipitate with $\mathrm{\mathrm{BaCl}_{2}}$ solution. The precipitate dissolves in dilute $\mathrm{\mathrm{HCl}}$ to produce a gas (B) which decolourises acidified $\mathrm{\mathrm{KMnO}_{4}}$ solution. A and $\mathrm{\mathrm{B}}$ respectively are
explanation:
$\rm{\begin{gathered}\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{3(a q)}+\mathrm{BaCl}_{2(a q)} \rightarrow \mathrm{BaSO}_{3} \underset{\text { (White ppt) }}{\downarrow}+2 \mathrm{NaCl}_{(a q)} \\\mathrm{BaSO}_{3(s)}+2 \mathrm{HCl}_{(\text {dil. })} \rightarrow \mathrm{BaCl}_{2}+\mathrm{SO}_{2(g)}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\end{gathered}}$
$\mathrm{\mathrm{BaCl}_{2}}$ is soluble in water, hence white precepitate disappears.
$\mathrm{5 \mathrm{SO}_{2}+2 \mathrm{MnO}_{4}^{-}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \rightarrow \underset{\text { colourless }}{5 \mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2-}+4 \mathrm{H}^{+}}}$
$\rm{+2 \mathrm{Mn}^{2+}}$
subject: Chemistry

question: The incorrect statement with respect to chromyl chloride test is
explanation:
Chromyl chloride test is used to confirm the presence of $\mathrm{\mathrm{Cl}^{-}}$in qualitative inorganic analysis. When a small amount of the substance (salt) is heated with conc. $\mathrm{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}}$ in presence of $\mathrm{\mathrm{K}_{2} \mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}}$, deep red vapours of chromyl chloride are evolved.
$\rm{\begin{gathered}\mathrm{NaCl}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4} \rightarrow \mathrm{NaHSO}_{4}+\mathrm{HCl} \\\mathrm{K}_{2} \mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{KHSO}_{4}+2 \mathrm{CrO}_{3}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \\\mathrm{CrO}_{3}+2 \mathrm{HCl} \rightarrow \mathrm{CrO}_{2} \mathrm{Cl}_{2}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\end{gathered}}$
Chromyl chloride
These vapours are passed through $\mathrm{\mathrm{NaOH}}$ solution and yellow coloured solution of $\mathrm{\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CrO}_{4}}$ is abtained.
$\mathrm{\mathrm{CrO}_{2} \mathrm{Cl}_{2}+4 \mathrm{NaOH} \rightarrow \mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CrO}_{4}+2 \mathrm{NaCl}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}}$
$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( {{\rm{Yellow}}\,\,{\rm{colour}}} \right)$
This yellow solution is neutralised with acetic acid and on addition of lead acetat gives a yellow ppt. of lead chromate.
${\rm{N}}{{\rm{a}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{Cr}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{4}}}{\rm{ + Pb}}{\left( {{\rm{C}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{3}}}{\rm{COO}}} \right)_{\rm{2}}} \to $
${\rm{PbCr}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{4}}}{\rm{ + 2C}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{3}}}{\rm{COONa}}$
$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{\rm{Yellow ppt}}{\rm{.}}$
subject: Chemistry

(Method for preparation of $\mathrm{\mathrm{KMnO}_{4}}$ )

question: What is the shape and magnetic nature of permanganate ion?
explanation:
The permanganate ions are tetrahedral. The $\mathrm{\pi}$ - bonding takes place by overlap of p-orbitals of oxygen with d-orbitals of manganese. It is also diamagnetic due to the absence of unpaired electron. The structure of permanganate ion is given below.

question: When a mixture of manganese dioxide, potassium hydroxide and potassium chlorate is fused, the product obtained is
explanation:
When a mixture of maganese dioxide, potassium hydroxide and potassium chlorate is fused then potassium chlorate first decomposes to give potassium chloride and oxygen gas. The formed $\mathrm{\mathrm{O}_{2}}$ gas then reacts with $\mathrm{\mathrm{MnO}_{2}}$ and $\mathrm{\mathrm{KOH}}$ to give $\mathrm{\mathrm{K}_{2} \mathrm{MnO}_{4}}$. The equations for the above reaction can be written as :
${\rm{2KCl}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{3}}}(\,{\rm{s}})\frac{\Delta }{{{\rm{Mn}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}}}}{\rm{2KCl}}({\rm{s}}){\rm{ + 3}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}}(\,{\rm{g}})$
${\rm{2Mn}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{ + 4KOH + }}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}} \to {\rm{2}}{{\rm{K}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{Mn}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{4}}}{\rm{ + 2}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{O}}$
subject: Chemistry

question: The ionic charges on chromate ion and dichromate ion respectively is
explanation:
The ionic charges on chromate and dichromate ion respectively is -2 and -2 . This can be predicted on the basis on the following structures :
Dichromate ion $\mathrm{\left(\mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}^{2-}\right)}$

explanation:
$\mathrm{\mathrm{K}_{2} \mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}+4 \mathrm{NaCl}+6 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4} \rightarrow}$ $\mathrm{2 \mathrm{CrO}_{2} \mathrm{Cl}_{2}+4 \mathrm{NaHSO}_{4}+2 \mathrm{KHSO}_{4}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}}$
Red vapours
$\mathrm{4 \mathrm{NaOH}+\mathrm{CrO}_{2} \mathrm{Cl}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CrO}_{4}+2 \mathrm{NaCl}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}}$ Yellow soln
${\rm{N}}{{\rm{a}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{Cr}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{4}}}{\rm{ + (C}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{3}}}{\rm{COO}}{{\rm{)}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{Pb}} \to \mathop {{\rm{PbCr}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{4}}}}\limits_{{\rm{yellow}}\,\,{\rm{ppt}}{\rm{.}}} {\rm{ + }}$
$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{\rm{2C}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{3}}}{\rm{COONa}}$

The vapours when passed into lead acetate in acetic acid gives a yellow precipitate.

The gas $\mathrm{\mathrm{B}}$ turns potassium dichromate acidified with dilute $\mathrm{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}}$ to a green coloured solution C. A, B and C are respectively
subject: Chemistry

question: When sulphur dioxide is passed in an acidified $\mathrm{\mathrm{K}_{2} \mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}}$ solution, the oxidation state of sulphur is changed from
explanation:
$\mathrm{\mathrm{K}_{2} \mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}}$ acts as a strong oxidising agent. It oxidises other compounds and gets itself reduced. In $\mathrm{\mathrm{SO}_{2}, \mathrm{~S}}$ is in +4 state so it gets oxidised to +6 state which is maximum group oxidation state.
$\mathrm{\mathrm{K}_{2} \mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}+4 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4} \rightarrow \mathrm{K}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}+\mathrm{Cr}_{2}\left(\mathrm{SO}_{4}\right)_{3}+4 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}}$
$\rm{\begin{array}{r}\dfrac{\left[\mathrm{SO}_{2}+[\mathrm{O}]+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \rightarrow \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}\right] \times 3}{\mathrm{~K}_{2} \mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}+3 \mathrm{SO}_{2} \rightarrow} \\\mathrm{K}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}+\mathrm{Cr}_{2}\left(\mathrm{SO}_{4}\right)_{3}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\end{array}}$
subject: Chemistry

question: The formula mass of Mohr's salt is 392. The iron present in it is oxidised by $\mathrm{\mathrm{KMnO}_{4}}$ in acid medium. The equivalent mass of Mohr's salt is
explanation:
Mohr's salt is $\mathrm{\left(\mathrm{NH}_{4}\right)_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4} \cdot \mathrm{FeSO}_{4} \cdot 6 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}}$. Oxidation of ferrous salt is as follows,
$\mathrm{5 \mathrm{Fe}^{2+}+\mathrm{MnO}_{4}^{-}+8 \mathrm{H}^{+} \rightarrow 5 \mathrm{Fe}^{3+}+\mathrm{Mn}^{2+}+4 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}}$
Total change in oxidation number of $\mathrm{\mathrm{Fe}=(+3)}$ $\mathrm{-(+2)=+1}$
Equivalent weight of Mohr's salt
$\rm{=\dfrac{\text { Molecular wt of Mohr's salt }}{1}=\dfrac{392}{1}=392}$
subject: Chemistry

question: The yellow precipitate formed during the chromyl chloride test is chemically
explanation:
Chromyl chloride test: When sodium chloride is heated with conc. $\mathrm{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}}$ in the presence of $\mathrm{\mathrm{K}_{2} \mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}}$, deep red vapours of chromyl chloride are evolved. These vapours are passed through $\mathrm{\mathrm{NaOH}}$ solution forms sodium chromate yellow colour solution. This solution neutralised with acetic acid and an addition of lead acetate gives yellow precipitate of lead chromate.
$\mathrm{\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CrO}_{4}+\mathrm{Pb}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COO}\right)_{2} \rightarrow}$ $\mathrm{\mathrm{PbCrO}_{4}+2 \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COONa}}$
subject: Chemistry

question: How is sodium chromate converted into sodium dichromate in the manufacture of potassium dichromate form chromite ore?
explanation:
By the action of concentrated sulphuric acid, sodium chromate gets converted into sodium dichromate in the manufacture of potassium dichromate.
Various steps involved are:
Step I concentration of the chromite ore by Wilfley method.
Step II Conversion of the concentrated chromite ore into $\mathrm{\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CrO}_{4}}$. Concentrated chromite ore is fused with molten $\mathrm{\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}}$ in the presence of air when $\mathrm{\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CrO}_{4}}$ and $\mathrm{\mathrm{Fe}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}}$ are formed.
Step III Conversion of $\mathrm{\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CrO}_{4}}$ into $\mathrm{\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}}$. Aqueous solution of $\mathrm{\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CrO}_{4}}$ is acidified to obtain $\mathrm{\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}}$.
${\text{N}}{{\text{a}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{Cr}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{4}}}{\text{ + }}\mathop {{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{S}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{4}}}}\limits_{\left( {{\text{Dil}}{\text{.}}} \right)} \xrightarrow{{}}{\text{N}}{{\text{a}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{C}}{{\text{r}}_{\text{2}}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{7}}}{\text{ + }}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{O}}$

options.text: By the action of concentrated sulphuric acid
By the action of sodium hydroxide

subject: Chemistry

question: The colour and magnetic nature of manganate ion $\mathrm{\left(\mathrm{MnO}_{4}^{2-}\right)}$ is
explanation: Oxidation state of $\mathrm{\mathrm{Mn}}$ in $\mathrm{\mathrm{MnO}_{4}^{2-}}$ is +6 . ${\rm{Mn( + 6) = 1}}{{\rm{s}}^{{\rm{2,}}}}{\rm{2}}{{\rm{s}}^{{\rm{2,}}}}{\rm{2}}{{\rm{p}}^{\rm{6}}}{\rm{,3}}{{\rm{s}}^{\rm{2}}}{\rm{3}}{{\rm{p}}^{\rm{6}}}{\rm{3\,}}{{\rm{d}}^{\rm{1}}}{\rm{,4}}{{\rm{s}}^{\rm{0}}}$ Hence, manganate ion $\mathrm{\left(\mathrm{MnO}_{4}^{2-}\right)}$ is paramagnetic due to presence of unpaired electron. Also, $\mathrm{\mathrm{MnO}_{4}^{2-}}$ is green in colour.
subject: Chemistry

explanation: Manganate $\mathrm{\left(\mathrm{MnO}_{4}^{2-}\right)}$ : https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764953360/preptracker/questions/q_25574_explanation_1764953359964.png $\mathrm{\Rightarrow \quad \pi}$-bonds are of $\mathrm{d \pi-p \pi}$ type https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764953361/preptracker/questions/q_25574_explanation_1764953360633.png $\mathrm{\Rightarrow \pi-}$ bonds are of $\mathrm{d \pi-p \pi}$ type

The $\mathrm{\pi}$-bonding involves overlap of $\mathrm{p}$ orbitals of oxygen with p-orbitals of manganese
The $\mathrm{\pi}$-bonding involves overlap of $\mathrm{d}$ orbitals of oxygen with d-orbitals of manganese

question: Which one of the following ions exhibits $\mathrm{\mathrm{d}-\mathrm{d}}$ transition and paramagnetism as well?
explanation:
$\mathrm{\mathrm{MnO}_{4}^{2-}}$
Oxidation number of $\mathrm{\mathrm{Mn}}$ is +6
$\mathrm{\operatorname{Mn}(25)=3 d^{5} 4 s^{2}}$
$\mathrm{M n^{6+}=3 d^{1}}$
It has one unpaired electron so it behaves as paramagnetic substance and also undergo $\mathrm{d-d}$ transition.

question: In which of the following, ions are correctly arranged in the increasing order of oxidizing power?
explanation:
The increasing order of oxidising power is $\mathrm{VO}_{2}^{+} < \mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}^{-2} < \mathrm{MnO}_{4}^{-}$.
The oxidation state of the various transition elements are $\mathrm{V}=+5, \mathrm{Mn}=+5, \mathrm{Mn}=+7$, $\mathrm{Cr}=+6$. Oxidizing power depends upon the stability of the reduction product obtained. Since lower oxides of Mn are more stable than that of Cr and V. So the order of increasing stability of the reduced species is $\mathrm{Mn}^{+2}>\mathrm{Cr}^{+3}>\mathrm{V}^{+3}$.
subject: Chemistry
topic: General characteristics of the transition elements
subtopic: Trends in the \[{{\text{M}}^{{\text{2 + }}}}{\text{/ M}}\] and \(\mathrm{M}^{3+}\)/\(\mathrm{M}^{2+}\) Standard Electrode Potentials

question:
Which of the following orders is correct for the property given?
(i) Ti (IV) is more stable than Ti (III) and Ti (II)
(ii) Among 3d-series elements (from Z=22 to 29) only copper has positive reduction potential $({{\text{M}}^{2 + }}/{\text{M)}}$
(iii) Both Sc and Zn exhibit $ + 1$ oxidation state
explanation:
(i) $\mathrm{Ti}=[\mathrm{Ar}] 3 \mathrm{~d}^{2} 4 \mathrm{~s}^{2}$
$\mathrm{Ti}^{3+}=[\mathrm{Ar}] 3 \mathrm{~d}^{1} 4 \mathrm{~s}^{0}$
$\mathrm{Ti}^{4+}=[\mathrm{Ar}] 3 \mathrm{~d}^{\circ} 4 \mathrm{~s}^{0}$
As $\mathrm{Ti}^{4+}$ have vacant d -orbital and it acquires nearest noble gas configuration so it is more stable as compared to $\mathrm{Ti}^{3+}$ which does not have vacant d - orbitals.
So, statement (i) is correct.
(ii) $\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{Cu}^{+2} / \mathrm{Cu}}^{\circ}=+0.34 \mathrm{~V}$ have exceptionally positive reduction potential.
So, statement (ii) is correct.
(iii) Sc shows $( + 3)$ oxidation state and Zn shows $ + 2$ oxidation state.
So, statement (iii) is incorrect.
So only (i) and (ii) are correct statements.
subject: Chemistry
topic: General characteristics of the transition elements
subtopic: Trends in Stability of Higher Oxidation States
parent_topic: General characteristics of the transition elements

question: Which of the following orders is correct for the property given?
explanation:
(1) Standard electrode potential value for $\mathrm{M}^{3+} / \mathrm{M}^{2+}$ is $\mathrm{Cr}>\mathrm{Fe}>\mathrm{Mn}$
(2) Magnetic moment is directly proportional to no. of unpaired electrons.
So, $\mathrm{Mn}^{2+}=3 \mathrm{~d}^{5}$ (5 unpaired electrons)
$\mathrm{Cr}^{2+}=3 \mathrm{~d}^{4}$ (4 unpaired electrons)
$\mathrm{Fe}^{2+}=3 \mathrm{~d}^{6}$ (4 unpaired electrons)
So, $\mathrm{Mn}^{2+}>\mathrm{Cr}^{2+} \approx \mathrm{Fe}^{2+}$
(3) $\mathop {{\rm{VO}}_2^ + }\limits^{( + 5)} < \mathop {{\rm{C}}{{\rm{r}}_2}}\limits^{( + 6)} {\rm{O}}_7^{2 - } < \mathop {{\rm{MnO}}_4^ - }\limits^{( + 7)} $
$\mathrm{Mn}^{+7}$ has highest oxidation state than $\mathrm{Cr}^{+6}$ and $\mathrm{V}^{+5}$ so it is acts as strongest oxidising agent.
(4) First ionisation enthalpy: $\mathrm{Ti}>\mathrm{Cr} < \mathrm{V}$
subject: Chemistry

question: Match the following properties of transition elements and choose the correct options from the codes given below.
explanation: Chromium acts as a reducing agent as it changes from a $\mathrm{d}^{4}$ to a $\mathrm{d}^{3}$ configuration, which is energetically more favourable. Manganese is an oxidising agent because the $\mathrm{d}^{5}$ configuration is highly stable. Transition metals are malleable due to the nature of metallic bonding, which allows atoms to slide over each other without breaking bonds. question:
Read the Assertion and Reason carefully to mark the correct option given below:

Assertion: Copper (Cu) has a unique positive standard electrode potential ($\mathrm{E}^{\circ}$) value.

Reason: The high energy required to convert solid copper to $\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}$ ions in solution is not compensated by the hydration enthalpy of $\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}$.
explanation: The unique positive standard electrode potential for copper is due to the higher energy required to convert copper to its $\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}$ ion, which is not balanced by the hydration enthalpy. Correct option is (1)

Both Assertion and Reason are true, but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.

Assertion is false, but Reason is true.

question:

Assertion: Transition elements show oxidation states that differ by one unit.

Reason: This variability is because d-block elements have incompletely filled d-orbitals, allowing for a range of stable oxidation states.
explanation: Transition elements have a variety of oxidation states due to their incompletely filled d-orbitals, allowing their oxidation states to change typically by one unit, which is in contrast to non-transition elements. Correct option is (1)

question:

explanation:

Hence, Assertion and Reason are true and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion.

question: $\mathrm{Cr}^{2+}$ and $\mathrm{Mn}^{3+}$ do possess $\mathrm{d}^{4}$ electronic configuration. So,
explanation: $\mathrm{Cr}^{2+}$ has  $3{{\text{d}}^4}$  configuration. It will lose electron to form  $3{{\text{d}}^3}$  configuration, which is stable (as it has half-filled  ${{\text{t}}_{2{\text{g}}}}$  level). Hence, it is a reducing agent. $\mathrm{Mn}^{3+}$ also has  $3{{\text{d}}^4}$  configuration. It will gain electron to form, $\mathrm{Mn}^{2+}$ which is stable  $3{{\text{d}}^5}$  configuration. Hence, it is an oxidising agent.

$\mathrm{Mn}^{3+}$ is reducing agent while $\mathrm{Cr}^{2+}$ is oxidising agent.

question: The compound with more covalent character in the following is
explanation:
According to Fajan's rule, smaller is the size of cation and more positive charge it has, stronger is the polarising power of that cation. Hence, that ion has a larger tendency to distort the electron cloud of anion. Hence, it has more covalent character.
The metal ions of $\mathrm{FeF}_{3}, \mathrm{VF}_{5}, \mathrm{VF}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{TiF}_{2}$ all belong to fourth period of periodic table. The metal ions exist as $\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}, \mathrm{V}^{5+}, \mathrm{V}^{2+}$ and $\mathrm{Ti}^{+2}$ respectively. The anions are same in all the compounds. The $\mathrm{V}^{+5}$ has the smallest size and largest charge. So the molecule $\mathrm{VF}_{5}$ has largest covalent character.

explanation:

The standard reduction potentials are
$\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{Z}=28) \Rightarrow-0.257 \mathrm{~V}$
$\mathrm{Cu}(\mathrm{Z}=29) \Rightarrow+0.337 \mathrm{~V}$
$\mathrm{Cr}(\mathrm{Z}=24) \Rightarrow-0.90 \mathrm{~V}$
$\mathrm{Ti}(\mathrm{Z}=21) \Rightarrow-1.63 \mathrm{~V}$

question:
$\mathrm{Co}^{+2}$ has higher magnetic moment than $\mathrm{Cr}^{+2}$.
Ionisation enthalpies of $\mathrm{Ce}, \mathrm{Pr}$ and Nd are higher than $\mathrm{Th}, \mathrm{Pa}, \mathrm{U}$.
Which of the following is correct?
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764958094/preptracker/questions/q_106957_explanation_1764958094066.png $\mu = \sqrt {{\rm{n}}({\rm{n}} + 2)} = \sqrt {3(3 + 2)} = \sqrt {15} = 3.95\,{\rm{B}}.{\rm{M}}$
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764958096/preptracker/questions/q_106957_explanation_1764958095585.png
$\mu = \sqrt {{\rm{n}}({\rm{n}} + 2)} = \sqrt {4(4 + 2)} = \sqrt {24} = 4.95\,{\rm{B}}.{\rm{M}}$
Statement (A) is not correct, as magnetic moment of $\mathrm{Cr}^{2+}$ is more than that of $\mathrm{Co}^{+2}$. But,
Statement (B) is correct, as I.P. of $\mathrm{Ce}, \mathrm{Pr}$ and Nd are higher than $\mathrm{Th}, \mathrm{Pa}$ and U . Thus, the correct option is (4).

options.text: Both  A  and  B  are not correct
Both  A  and  B  are correct
A  is correct but  B  is not correct
A  is not correct but  B  is correct

question:
Given below are two statements :
Ferromagnetism is considered as an extreme form of paramagnetism.
The number of unpaired electrons in a $\mathrm{Cr}^{2+}$ ion $(\mathrm{Z}=24)$ is the same as that of a $\mathrm{Nd}^{3+}$ ion $(\mathrm{Z}=60)$.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below:
explanation:
Paramagnetism is the ability to attract magnets. Substances which are attracted very strongly in applied magnetic field are termed as ferromagnetic. In fact, ferromagnetism is an extreme form of paramagnetism.
Hence statement A is correct.
$\mathrm{Cr}^{2+}=3 \mathrm{~d}^{4} 4 \mathrm{~s}^{0}$; Unpaired electrons $=4$
$\mathrm{Nd}^{3+}=4 \mathrm{f}^{3} 6 \mathrm{~s}^{0}$; Unpaired electrons $=3$
Hence, Statement B is incorrect

options.text: Statement A  is false but Statement B is true
Both Statement A and Statement B are true
Both Statement A  and Statement B are false
Statement A  is true but Statement B is false

question:
Which of the following statements are correct about ${\mathrm{\mathrm{Zn}, \mathrm{Cd}}}$ and Hg ?
(I) They exhibit high enthalpy of atomization as the d-subshell is full.
(II) Zn and Cd do not show variable oxidation state while Hg shows +I and +II.
(III) Compounds of ${\mathrm{\mathrm{Zn}, \mathrm{Cd}}}$ and Hg are paramagnetic in nature.
(IV) ${\mathrm{\mathrm{Zn}, \mathrm{Cd}}}$ and Hg are called soft metals.
Choose the most appropriate from the options given below :
explanation:
Due to absence of unpaired electrons in Zn , Cd and Hg , these elements exhibits low enthalpy of atomization.
Zn and Cd do not show variable oxidation states because they both have completely filled d-orbitals.
Compounds of ${\mathrm{\mathrm{Zn}, \mathrm{Cd}}}$ and Hg are diamagnetic in nature as there are no unpaired electrons in these metals.
${\mathrm{\mathrm{Zn}, \mathrm{Cd}}}$ and Hg are called soft metals because d-electrons do not participate in metallic bonding.
So, the correct option is (1).
subject: Chemistry

parent_topic: General characteristics of the transition elements

question:
Read the Assertion and Reason carefully to mark the correct option out of the options given below.

Assertion: In aqueous solutions ${\mathrm{\mathrm{Cr}^{2+}}}$ is reducing while ${\mathrm{\mathrm{Mn}^{3+}}}$ is oxidising in nature.

Reason: Extra stability to half filled electronic configuration is observed than incompletely filled electronic configuration.
explanation:
${\mathrm{\mathrm{Cr}^{2+}}}$ is reducing as its configuration changes from ${\mathrm{\mathrm{d}^{4}}}$ to ${\mathrm{\mathrm{d}^{3}}}$, the latter having a half-filled ${\mathrm{\mathrm{t}_{2 g}}}$ level. On the other hand, the change from ${\mathrm{\mathrm{Mn}^{3+}}}$ to ${\mathrm{\mathrm{Mn}^{2+}}}$ results in the half-filled ${\mathrm{\left(\mathrm{d}^{5}\right)}}$ configuration which has extra stability.
So, the correct option is (1).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

question:
Read the Statement - A and Statement - B carefully to mark the correct options given below
The higher oxidation states are more stable down the group among transition elements unlike
p -block elements.
Copper cannot liberate hydrogen from weak acids.
explanation:
In transition elements on moving down the group the stability of higher oxidation state increases. This is due to similarity of energy of the d-orbitals with the outer s-orbitals.
While in case of p-block elements stability of lower oxidation state increases down the group due to iner pair effect. Hence statement ${\mathrm{A}}$ is correct.
Statement B is also true as ${\mathrm{\mathrm{Cu} \operatorname{SRP}}}$ value is ${\mathrm{+\mathrm{ve}(0.34 \mathrm{~V})}}$. It cannot liberate hydrogen with acids, as it lies below hydrogen in electrochemical series.
So, the correct option is (3).

options.text: Statement A is correct but Statement B is incorrect.
Statement A is incorrect but Statement B is correct.

subject: Chemistry
topic: General characteristics of the transition elements
parent_topic: General characteristics of the transition elements

question: The electronic configuration of ${\mathrm{\mathrm{Cu}(\mathrm{II})}}$ is ${\mathrm{3 \mathrm{~d}^{9}}}$ whereas that of ${\mathrm{\mathrm{Cu}(\mathrm{I})}}$ is ${\mathrm{3 \mathrm{~d}^{10}}}$. Which of the following is correct?
explanation:
Copper (I) compounds are unstable in aqueous solution and undergo disproportionation.
$2 \mathrm{Cu}^{+} \rightarrow \mathrm{Cu}^{2+}+\mathrm{Cu}$
The stability of ${\mathrm{\mathrm{Cu}_{(\text {aq })}^{2+}}}$ is due to much more negative ${\mathrm{\Delta_{\text {hyd }} \mathrm{H}^{\circ}}}$ of ${\mathrm{\mathrm{Cu}_{\text {(aq) }}^{2+}}}$ than ${\mathrm{\mathrm{Cu}_{\text {(aq) }}^{+}}}$.
It is more significant than fulfilled electronic configuration. So, the correct option is (1).

options.text: Stability of ${\mathrm{\mathrm{Cu}(\mathrm{I})}}$ and ${\mathrm{\mathrm{Cu}(}}$ II ${\mathrm{)}}$ depends on nature of copper salts.

question:
The number of ions from the following that have the ability to liberate hydrogen from a dilute acid is ____.
$\mathrm{Ti}^{2+}, \mathrm{Cr}^{2+} \text { and } \mathrm{V}^{2+}$
explanation:
$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{\rm{Ti}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{\rm{V}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{\rm{Cr}}$
${{\rm{E}}^{\rm{^\circ }}}\left( {{{\rm{M}}^{{\rm{3 + }}}}{\rm{/}}{{\rm{M}}^{{\rm{2 + }}}}} \right)\quad - {\rm{0}}.{\rm{37}}\quad - {\rm{0}}.{\rm{26}}\quad - {\rm{0}}.{\rm{41}}$
${\rm{(in}}\,{\rm{volts)}}$
The above ${\mathrm{\mathrm{E}^{\circ}}}$ values indicate that ${\mathrm{\mathrm{Ti}^{2+}, \mathrm{V}^{2+}}}$ and ${\mathrm{\mathrm{Cr}^{2+}}}$ ions are strong reducing agents and can liberate hydrogen from a dilute acid. So, the correct option is (3).

question: A first row transition metal in its +2 oxidation state has a spin-only magnetic moment value of 3.86 BM. The atomic number of the metal is
explanation:
${\mathrm{\mu=\sqrt{\mathrm{n}(\mathrm{n}+2)}=3.86 \mathrm{BM}}}$
On solving, ${\mathrm{\mathrm{n}=3}}$
$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{\rm{[n = No}}{\rm{.of}}\,{\rm{unpaired}}\,{\rm{electrons]}}$
Electronic configuration of ion in +2 oxidation state should be ${\mathrm{[\mathrm{Ar}] 3 \mathrm{~d}^{3}}}$.
Thus electronic configuration of atom will be ${\mathrm{[\mathrm{Ar}] 3 \mathrm{~d}^{3} 4 \mathrm{~s}^{2}}}$ (Vanadium).
${\mathrm{\therefore}}$ Atomic number of the metal is 23 .So, the correct option is (2).

${\rm{N}}{{\rm{i}}^{{\rm{2 + }}}}:{\rm{n}} = 2,{\rm{M}}{{\rm{n}}^{{\rm{2 + }}}}:{\rm{n}} = 5$

question: Why is oxygen superior to fluorine in stabilizing higher oxidation states of transition metals?

Because of larger size of oxygen as compared to fluorine
Because of the ability of oxygen to form multiple bonds with metals

question:

(i) Manganese exhibits $+ 7$ oxidation state in its oxide.
(ii) Ruthenium and Osmium exhibit $+ 8$ oxidation state in their oxides.
(iii) Sc shows $ + \,4$ oxidation state which is oxidizing in nature.
(iv) Cr shows oxidising nature in $+ 6$ oxidation state.
explanation:
$ \bullet $ Mn shows $+ 7$ oxidation state in $\mathrm{Mn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}$
$ \bullet $ Ruthenium and Osmium exhibit +8 oxidation state.
$ \bullet $ Maximum oxidation state of Sc is $+ 3$ .

question:
Which of the following statements are correct?
(A) The $\mathrm{M}^{3+} / \mathrm{M}^{2+}$ reduction potential for iron is greater than manganese.
(B) The higher oxidation states of first row d-block elements get stabilized by oxide ion.
(C) Aqueous solution of $\mathrm{Cr}^{2+}$ can liberate hydrogen from dilute acid.
(D) Magnetic moment of $\mathrm{V}^{2+}$ is observed between 4.4 - 5.2 B.M.
Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
explanation:
$ \bullet $ The $\mathrm{M}^{3+} / \mathrm{M}^{2+}$ reduction potential of iron and manganese are 0.77 V and 1.57 V , respectively.
$ \bullet $ Higher oxidation states of first row d-block elements are stabilised by atoms of high electronegativities like O and F .
$ \bullet $ $\mathrm{Cr}^{2+}$ is highly reactive in its aqueous solution, hence can liberate hydrogen from dilute acid.
$ \bullet $ Magnetic moment of $\mathrm{V}^{2+}$ is observed to be 3.87 B.M.
Therefore, only statements (B) and (C) are correct.

question:
Assertion: Reduction potential of ${\text{Mn( - 3}}\,\,{\text{to 2)}}$ is more positive than ${\text{Fe( + 3}}\,\,{\text{to + 2)}}{\text{.}}$

Reason: Ionisation potential of $\mathrm{Mn}$ is more than that of $\mathrm{Fe}$.
explanation:
$\mathrm{Mn}^{2+}=[\mathrm{Ar}] 3 \mathrm{~d}^{5}, \mathrm{Mn}^{3+}=[\mathrm{Ar}] 3 \mathrm{~d}^{4}$
$\mathrm{Fe}^{2+}=[\mathrm{Ar}] 3 \mathrm{~d}^{6}, \mathrm{Fe}^{3+}=[\mathrm{Ar}] 3 \mathrm{~d}^{5}$
Thus, $\mathrm{Mn}^{2+}$ has more stable configuration than $\mathrm{Mn}^{3+}$ while $\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}$ has more stable configuration than $\mathrm{Fe}^{2+}$. Hence, reduction potential for $\mathrm{Mn}^{3+} / \mathrm{Mn}^{2+}$ couple is more positive than $\mathrm{Fe}^{3+} / \mathrm{Fe}^{2+}$.
As we move across the period, ionisation potential increases, thus, ionisation potential of $\mathrm{Fe}(762 \mathrm{KJ} / \mathrm{mol})$ is more than that of $\mathrm{Mn}(717$ $\mathrm{KJ} / \mathrm{mol}$ ). So option (3) is correct.

question:
Assertion: Transition metals are poor reducing agents.


Reason: Transition metals form numerous alloys with other metals.
explanation: In actual practice, transition metals react with acids very slowly and act as poor reducing agents. This is due to the protection of metal as a result of formation of thin oxide protective film. Further, their poor tendency as reducing agent is due to high ionisation energy, high heat of vapourisation and low heat of hydration. Transition metals form alloys due to similar size. So option (2) is correct.

So option (1) is correct.

question:
Assertion: Cuprous ion $\left(\mathrm{Cu}^{+}\right)$is colourless whereas cupric ion $\left(\mathrm{Cu}^{++}\right)$is blue in aqueous solution.


Reason: Cuprous ion $\left(\mathrm{Cu}^{+}\right)$has unpaired electrons while cupric ion $\left(\mathrm{Cu}^{++}\right)$does not.
explanation:
$\begin{aligned}& \mathrm{Cu}^{+}:[\mathrm{Ar}] 3 \mathrm{~d}^{10} \\& \mathrm{Cu}^{2+}:[\mathrm{Ar}] 3 \mathrm{~d}^{9}\end{aligned}$
Cuprous ion $\left(\mathrm{Cu}^{+}\right)$is colourless because it has no unpaired electrons in the subshell $3 \mathrm{~d}$ as it has $3 \mathrm{~d}^{10}$ configuration. Cupric ion $\left(\mathrm{Cu}^{++}\right)$has one unpaired electron as it has
$3 \mathrm{~d}^{9}$ configuration. By absorbing the visible light, the unpaired electron can migrate from one set of d-orbitals to another. Hence, cupric ion $\left(\mathrm{Cu}^{++}\right)$in aqueous solution shows blue colour. So option (3) is correct.

question:

Reason: Standard oxidation potential of $\mathrm{Cu}$ is below $\mathrm{H}$.
explanation:
Copper has its standard oxidation potential below $\mathrm{H}$ and thus cannot liberate $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ from $\mathrm{HCl}$. However, it dissolves in concentrated nitric acid because conc. $\mathrm{HNO}_{3}$ is strong oxidising agent.
$\mathrm{Cu}+4 \mathrm{HNO}_{3} \rightarrow \mathrm{Cu}\left(\mathrm{NO}_{3}\right)_{2}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+2 \mathrm{NO}_{2}$
So option (1) is correct.

question:
Assertion: Rusting of iron is an example of corrosion.

Reason: Rusting of iron is decreased by acids and electrolyte.
explanation: Corrosion is a process through which refined metals are converted into more stable compounds such as metal oxides, metal sulfides, or metal hydroxides. Rusting of iron is a common example. During rusting, iron oxidizes to $\mathrm{Fe}^{2+}$ ions. These ions combine to form $\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}$, which further oxidizes to produce $\mathrm{Fe}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3} \cdot \mathrm{xH}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ (rust). Acids aid in the dissolution of pure iron to ferrous ions, while electrolytes enhance conductivity, facilitating the electrochemical reactions involved in rust formation. Therefore option (3) is correct.

$\mathrm{CuSO}_{4} .5 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ is commonly called blue vitriol.
It is blue in colour due to the presence of 5 molecules of water of crystallization. So option (2) is correct.

$\mathrm{HgCl}_{2}$ sublimes on heating. $\mathrm{SnCl}_{2}$ is strong reducing agent and reduces $\mathrm{HgCl}_{2}$ first to $\mathrm{Hg}_{2} \mathrm{Cl}_{2}$ (white) and then to $\mathrm{Hg}$ (black).
$\begin{aligned}& \mathrm{SnCl}_{2}+2 \mathrm{HgCl}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{SnCl}_{4}+\underset{\text { (White) }}{\mathrm{Hg}_{2} \mathrm{Cl}_{2}} \\& \mathrm{SnCl}_{2}+\mathrm{Hg}_{2} \mathrm{Cl}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{SnCl}_{4}+\underset{\text { (Black) }}{2 \mathrm{Hg}} .\end{aligned}$
So, $\mathrm{HgCl}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{SnCl}_{2}$ cannot exist together in an aqueous solution. So option (3) is correct.

explanation:
Oxidation state of ${\text{d}}$ - block elements can vary, i.e. of $\mathrm{Fe}, \mathrm{Co}$ and $\mathrm{Cr}$ as follows:
(1) (3) Oxidation state of $\mathrm{Co}=(+) 2$ to $(+) 5$ and oxidation state of $\mathrm{Fe}=(+) 2$ to $(+) 6$, but $(+) 6$ is less stable. Also $(+) 4$ and +5 oxidation states of $\mathrm{Fe}$ are very rare.
(4) Oxidation state of $\mathrm{Cr}=(+) 2$ to $(+) 6$, and
$(+) 6$ state is more stable.
(2) Oxidation state of alkaline earth metals (i.e. $\mathrm{Mg})$ is fixed and equal to $(+) 2$.
Hence, option (4) is the correct.
subject: Chemistry
topic: General characteristics of the transition elements
parent_topic: General characteristics of the transition elements

question: The correct order of spin only magnetic moment (in $\mathrm{BM}$ ) for $\mathrm{Mn}^{2+}, \mathrm{Cr}^{2+}$ and $\mathrm{Ti}^{2+}$ ions is
explanation:
Spin only magnetic moment depends upon the number of unpaired electrons. Magnetic moment,
$\mu=\sqrt{\mathrm{n}(\mathrm{n}+2)}$
where, $\mathrm{n}=$ number of unpaired electrons
So, higher is the no.of unpaired electrons, higher will be magnetic moment.
$\mathrm{Mn}^{+2} \rightarrow[\mathrm{Ar}] 3 \mathrm{~d}^{5}$ (5 unpaired electrons)
$\mathrm{Cr}^{+2} \rightarrow[\mathrm{Ar}] 3 \mathrm{~d}^{4}$ (4 unpaired electrons)
$\mathrm{Ti}^{+2} \rightarrow[\mathrm{Ar}] 3 \mathrm{~d}^{2}$ (2 unpaired electrons)
$\therefore$ Correct order of spin only magnetic moment is $\mathrm{Mn}^{2+}>\mathrm{Cr}^{2+}>\mathrm{Ti}^{2+}$.
subject: Chemistry
topic: General characteristics of the transition elements
parent_topic: General characteristics of the transition elements

question: Magnetic moment of $\left[\mathrm{Ti}\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right)_{6}\right]^{3+}$ is
explanation:
$\left[\mathrm{Ti}\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right)_{6}\right]^{3+}$
$\operatorname{Ti}(22)=[\operatorname{Ar}] 4 \mathrm{~s}^{2} 3 \mathrm{~d}^{2}$
$\mathrm{Ti}^{3+}=[\mathrm{Ar}] 4 \mathrm{~s}^{0} 3 \mathrm{~d}^{1}$ (1 unpaired electron)
$\therefore \mu=\sqrt{\mathrm{n}(\mathrm{n}+2)}=\sqrt{1(1+2)}=1.73 \mathrm{BM}$
subject: Chemistry
topic: General characteristics of the transition elements
parent_topic: General characteristics of the transition elements

question: For which of the following pairs, magnetic moment is same?
explanation:
Species having the same number of unpaired electrons, have same magnetic moment.
(1) $\mathrm{MnCl}_{2} \Rightarrow \mathrm{Mn}^{2+}=[\mathrm{Ar}] 3 \mathrm{~d}^{5}, 4 \mathrm{~s}^{0}$
(five unpaired electrons)
$\mathrm{CuSO}_{4} \Rightarrow \mathrm{Cu}^{2+}=[\mathrm{Ar}] 3 \mathrm{~d}^{9}, 4 \mathrm{~s}^{0}$
(one unpaired electron)
(2) $\mathrm{CuCl}_{2} \Rightarrow \mathrm{Cu}^{2+}=[\mathrm{Ar}] 3 \mathrm{~d}^{9}, 4 \mathrm{~s}^{0}$
(one unpaired electron)
$\mathrm{TiCl}_{3} \Rightarrow \mathrm{Ti}^{3+}=[\mathrm{Ar}] 3 \mathrm{~d}^{1}, 4 \mathrm{~s}^{0}$
(one unpaired electron)
(3) $\mathrm{TiO}_{2} \Rightarrow \mathrm{Ti}^{4+}=[\mathrm{Ar}] 3 \mathrm{~d}^{0}, 4 \mathrm{~s}^{0}$
(no unpaired electron)
$\mathrm{CuSO}_{4} \Rightarrow \mathrm{Cu}^{2+}=[\mathrm{Ar}] 3 \mathrm{~d}^{9}, 4 \mathrm{~s}^{0}$
(one unpaired electron)
(4) $\mathrm{TiCl}_{3} \Rightarrow \mathrm{Ti}^{3+}=[\mathrm{Ar}] 3 \mathrm{~d}^{1}, 4 \mathrm{~s}^{0}$
(one unpaired electron)
$\mathrm{NiCl}_{2} \Rightarrow \mathrm{Ni}^{2+}=[\mathrm{Ar}] 3 \mathrm{~d}^{8}, 4 \mathrm{~s}^{0}$
(two unpaired electrons)
$\therefore \mathrm{CuCl}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{TiCl}_{3}$ will have same magnetic moment.
$\therefore \mu = \sqrt {1(1 + 2)} = \sqrt {\text{3}} = 1.73\,\,{\text{BM}}$
subject: Chemistry
topic: General characteristics of the transition elements
parent_topic: General characteristics of the transition elements

question: Which of the following compounds is coloured?
explanation:
The compounds having metal ions with unpaired electrons, are coloured. Thus,
(1) In $\mathrm{TiCl}_{3}, \mathrm{Ti}^{3+}=[\mathrm{Ar}] 3 \mathrm{~d}^{1}$
(one unpaired electron)
(2) In $\mathrm{FeCl}_{3}, \mathrm{Fe}^{3+}=[\mathrm{Ar}] 3 \mathrm{~d}^{5}$
(Five unpaired electrons)
(3) In $\mathrm{CoCl}_{2}, \mathrm{Co}^{2+}=[\mathrm{Ar}] 3 \mathrm{~d}^{7}$
(three unpaired electrons)
Hence, all compounds given are coloured.

question: Identify the oxidation states of titanium $\mathrm{(Z=22)}$ and copper $\mathrm{(Z=29)}$ in their colourless compounds.
explanation:
Electronic configuration of titanium (Ti) $\mathrm{(\mathrm{Z}=22)}$ is ${{\rm{[Ar]}}_{{\rm{18}}}}{\rm{3}}{{\rm{d}}^{\rm{2}}}{\rm{4}}{{\rm{s}}^{\rm{2}}}$. In case of $\mathrm{\mathrm{Ti}^{4+}}$, the electronic configuration will be ${{\rm{[Ar]}}_{{\rm{18}}}}{\rm{3}}{{\rm{d}}^{\rm{0}}}{\rm{4}}{{\rm{s}}^{\rm{0}}}$
Similarly, electronic configuration of copper $\mathrm{(\mathrm{Cu})}$ $\mathrm{(Z=29)}$ is $\mathrm{[A r]_{18} 3 d^{10} 4 s^{1}}$. On loosing one electron it acquires stable electronic configuration, i.e ${{\rm{[Ar]}}_{{\rm{18}}}}{\rm{3}}{{\rm{d}}^{{\rm{10}}}}$.
In both ${\rm{T}}{{\rm{i}}^{{\rm{4 + }}}}$ and $\mathrm{\mathrm{Cu}^{+}}$no unpaired electron is present. As a result, no d-d transition occur and therefore these are colourless in nature.

question: For the four successive transition elements $\mathrm{(\mathrm{Cr}}$, $\mathrm{\mathrm{Mn}, \mathrm{Fe}}$ and $\mathrm{\mathrm{Co}}$ ), the stability of +2 oxidation state will be there in which of the following order?
explanation: $\mathrm{\mathrm{Mn}^{2+}\left(d^{5}\right)}$ gets higest stabilisation due to halffilled configuration. In case of $\mathrm{\mathrm{Fe}^{2+}\left(\mathrm{d}^{6}\right)}$, addition of one extra electron in the subshell destabilises it. Addition of two electrons in case of $\mathrm{\mathrm{Co}^{2+}\left(d^{7}\right)}$ destabilises it more. $\mathrm{\mathrm{Cr}^{2+}\left(d^{4}\right)}$ has one vacant subshell. $\mathrm{\mathrm{Fe}^{2+}}$ gets more stabilisation as compared to $\mathrm{\mathrm{Cr}}$ through exchange energy. So correct order is $\mathrm{\mathrm{Mn}>\mathrm{Fe}>\mathrm{Cr}>\mathrm{Co}}$.

question: The variability of oxidation state
explanation: The variability of oxidation states is a characterstic of transition elements, arises out of incomplete filling of d-orbitals in such a way that their oxidation states differ from each other by unity, e.g., $\mathrm{\mathrm{V}^{\mathrm{II}}, \mathrm{V}^{\mathrm{III}}, \mathrm{V}^{\mathrm{IV}}, \mathrm{V}^{\mathrm{V}}}$. This is in contrast with the variability of oxidation states of non-transition elements where oxidation states normally differ by a unit of two.

options.text: is a characteristic of transition elements
arises out of incomplete filling of d-orbitals in such a way that their oxidation states differ from each other by unity
it differs from the variability of oxidation states of non-transition elements where the oxidation states normally differ by a unit of two.

Identify the correct statements from the above mentioned.
explanation: When an electron from a lower energy d-orbital is excited to a higher energy d-orbital, the energy of excitation corresponds to the frequency of light absorbed. This frequency generally lies in the visible region. The colour observed corresponds to the complementary colour of the light absorbed. The frequency of light absorbed is determined by the nature of the ligand.

question:
Assertion: The order of atomic radii of $\mathrm{\mathrm{Cu}, \mathrm{Ag}}$ and $\mathrm{\mathrm{Au}}$ is $\mathrm{\mathrm{Cu} < \mathrm{Ag} \approx \mathrm{Au}}$.


Reason: The atomic radii of $\mathrm{4 d}$ series elements are larger than those of $\mathrm{3 d}$ series elements but generally the radii of $\mathrm{4 d}$ and $\mathrm{5 d}$ series elements are almost identical.
explanation: The atomic radii, in general, increase down the group. The atomic radii of second series are larger than those of first transition series. In the atoms of the second transition series, the number of shells are more than those of the first transition series. As a result, the atoms of second transition series, the number of shells are more than those of the first transition series. As a result, the atoms of second transition series are larger than those of the elements of the first transition series. But the atomic radii of the second and third transition series are almost the same. This phenomenon is associated with the intervention of the $\mathrm{4 \mathrm{f}}$ orbitals which must be filled before the ${\rm{5d}}$ series of elements begin. The filling of $\mathrm{4 \mathrm{f}}$ before ${\rm{5d}}$ orbital results in a regular decrease in atomic radii called Lanthanoid contraction which essentially compensates for the expected increase in atomic size with increasing atomic number.

question: Identify the correct decreasing order of densities of d-block elements.
explanation:
The correct order of densities of d-block elements are as follows :
$\rm{\mathrm{Ni}>\mathrm{Fe}>\mathrm{Cr}>\mathrm{V}}$
On moving across a period electrons have the same principal quantum number and so they are not making the atom bigger, but at the same time there is more protons and neutrons resulting increase in number of nucleous and due to effectively increase in charge of nucleus there is decreases in radius of the atom.
$\rm{\text { Density }=\dfrac{\text { mass }}{\text { volume }}}$
Mass is increasing and volume is decreasing, overall we conclude that on moving left to right density increases.
subject: Chemistry

explanation: In any row the melting points of transition metals rise to a maximum at $\mathrm{d^{5}}$ except for anomalous values of $\mathrm{\mathrm{Mn}}$ and Te and falls regularly as the atomic number increases.

Assertion: Mo has the ground state electronic configuration $4{{\rm{d}}^5}\,\,5\,{{\rm{s}}^1}.$

Mo atomic number is 42 , so the ground state electronic configuration can be written as $[\mathrm{Kr}] 4 \mathrm{~d}^{5} 5 \mathrm{~s}^{1}$. So assertion is true.
Exchange energy is energy liberated when 2 or more electrons with similar spin exchange their positions in the degenerate orbitals. More exchange energy is noticed for $\mathrm{d}^{10}$, so, reason is incorrect.

$\begin{aligned}& { }_{24} \mathrm{Cr} \rightarrow[\mathrm{Ar}] 4 \mathrm{~s}^{1} 3 \mathrm{~d}^{5}\left(6 \text { unpaired } \mathrm{e}^{-}\right) \\& { }_{23} \mathrm{~V} \rightarrow[\mathrm{Ar}] 4 \mathrm{~s}^{2} 3 \mathrm{~d}^{3}\left(3 \text { unpaired } \mathrm{e}^{-}\right) \\& { }_{22} \mathrm{Ti} \rightarrow[\mathrm{Ar}] 4 \mathrm{~s}^{2} 3 \mathrm{~d}^{2}\left(2 \text { unpaired } \mathrm{e}^{-}\right) \\& { }_{25} \mathrm{Mn} \rightarrow[\mathrm{Ar}] 4 \mathrm{~s}^{2} 3 \mathrm{~d}^{5}\left(5 \text { unpaired } \mathrm{e}^{-}\right)\end{aligned}$
So, the correct option is (1).

question: ' ${\mathrm{X}}$ ' and ' ${\mathrm{Y}}$ ' are d-block elements placed in group 12 of the modern periodic table. ' ${\mathrm{X}}$ ' is in period 4 and ' Y ' is in period 5. How many elements are placed between ' X ' and ' Y ' in the modern periodic table?
explanation:
Element ' X ' in group 12 and period 4 is Zn with atomic number 30 .
After ' Zn ' in period 4 , there are $\underline {{\text{6}}\,{\text{p - block}}} $ elements. After these elements, period 5 stars with $\underline {{\text{2 s - blocks}}} $, followed by $\underline {{\text{10 d - block elements}}} $. However, ' Y ' is placed in group 12 and period 5. So, ' ${\mathrm{Y}}$ ' is last d-block element of period 5 . So, when counting the elements between ' ${\mathrm{X}}$ ' and ' ${\mathrm{Y}}$ ', we need to exclude ' ${\mathrm{Y}}$ '.
${\mathrm{\therefore \quad}}$ No. of elements between ' X ' and ' Y ' ${\mathrm{=6+2+9=17}}$
subject: Chemistry

question: Among the following, the one that is not considered as a typical transition element.
explanation: By definition, a transition element contain an incompletely filled d-subshell. With its $\mathrm{3 d^{10}}$ configuration, zinc does not fall under this definition. Transition elements are metallic solids with high melting and boiling points. Zinc has a melting point of only $\mathrm{420^{\circ} \mathrm{C}}$ and a relatively low boiling point of $\mathrm{907^{\circ} \mathrm{C}}$. Zinc is also a metallic solid, which forms a variety of complexes like other transition elements. Hence zinc is not considered as a typical transition element.
subject: Chemistry

question: The electronic configuration of actinoids cannot be assigned with degree of certainty because of
explanation: For the first four actinide elements, $\mathrm{Th}, \mathrm{Pa}, \mathrm{U}$ and $\mathrm{Np}$, the difference in energy between $5\,{\text{f}}$ and $6\,{\text{d}}$-orbitals is small. Thus, in these elements (and their ions) electrons may occupy the $5\,{\text{f}}$ or the $6\,{\text{d}}$ levels or sometimes both. Later in the actinide series, the 5 -orbitals become appreciably lower in energy. Thus, from $\mathrm{Pu}$ onwards the 5 -shell fills in a regular way and the elements become very similar.

options.text: small energy difference between 5f and 6d levels
free movement of electrons over all the orbitals

question: If M is element of actinoids series, the degree of complex formation decreases in the order
explanation:
The higher the charge on the metal ion, smaller is the ionic size and more is the complex forming ability. Thus, the degree of complex formation decreases in the order :

question: The number of f -electrons in  $ + 3$  oxidation state of gadolinium $({\rm{Z}} = 64)$  is ${\rm{x}}$  and in  $ + 2$  oxidation state of ytterbium  $({\rm{Z}} = 70)$  is  ${\rm{y}}$. The sum of  ${\rm{x}}$  and  ${\rm{y}}$  is
explanation:
The electronic configuration of,
${\text{G}}{{\text{d}}_{64}} = [{\text{Xe}}]4{{\text{f}}^7}5{{\text{d}}^1}6{{\text{s}}^2}$ and
$\mathrm{Gd}^{3+}=[\mathrm{Xe}] 4 \mathrm{f}^{7} \Rightarrow \mathrm{x}=7$
Electronic configuration of, $\mathrm{Yb}_{70}=[\mathrm{Xe}] 4 \mathrm{f}^{14} 6 \mathrm{~s}^{2}$ and $\mathrm{Yb}^{2+}=[\mathrm{Xe}] 4 \mathrm{f}^{14} \Rightarrow \mathrm{y}=14$
$\therefore \quad \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{y}=7+14=21$

question:
Identify all the correct statements for lanthanoide contraction.
(A) The covalent properties of the lanthanoide metal hydroxides increases from La to Lu .
(B) The chemical reactivity decreases from La to Lu .
(C) $\mathrm{La}(\mathrm{OH})_{3}$ is more basic than $\mathrm{Lu}(\mathrm{OH})_{3}$.
(D) Zr and Hf have about the same radius.
(E) Separation of lanthanoids from one another is easy.
explanation:
(A) On moving from La to Lu , size of cation decreases due to poor shielding of f -orbitals. This contraction is called lanthanoid contraction. As size of cation decreases, its polarising power increases thus, covalent character or properties of lanthanoid metal hydroxides increases.
(B) The first few lanthanoids are quite reactive like calcium but with increasing atomic number, their reactivity becomes similar to Al, i.e. reactivity decreases. This is because of lanthanoid contraction. The ionisation becomes difficult as size of atom decreases.
Hence, chemical reactivity decreases from La to Lu .
(C) From La to Lu , size of metal cation decreases, i.e. covalent character in metal hydroxides increases and basic strength decreases. Hence, $\mathrm{La}(\mathrm{OH})_{3}$ is more basic than $\mathrm{Lu}(\mathrm{OH})_{3}$.
(D) On moving down the group from Zr to Hf , size of atom should increases. But due to lanthanoide contraction, Zr and Hf have almost same radius. This can explained on the basis of shielding effect. Due to presence of f -orbitals and their poor shielding, effective nuclear charge on the outer shell electron increases and as a result, size of Zr and Hf becomes equal.
(E) Due to lanthanoid contraction, the ionic radii of lanthanoids decreases which also affect their chemistry. Thus, the separation of lanthanoids from one another is not easy.
subject: Chemistry

question: The correct order of ionic radii of trivalent ions $\mathrm{Y}^{3+}, \mathrm{La}^{3+}, \mathrm{Eu}^{3+}$ and $\mathrm{Lu}^{3+}$ is  $({\text{Y}} = 39,{\text{La}} = 57,{\text{Eu}} = 63,{\text{Lu}} = 71)$
explanation:
On moving down the group, size of ion increases thus, $\mathrm{Y}^{3+}$ is smallest in size than $\mathrm{La}^{3+}, \mathrm{Lu}^{3+}$ and $\mathrm{Eu}^{3+}$.
On moving left to right in lanthanoids, size of cation decreases because of lanthanoid contraction. Hence, the correct order of ionic radii is $\mathrm{Y}^{3+} < \mathrm{Lu}^{3+} < \mathrm{Eu}^{3+} < \mathrm{La}^{3+}$.
subject: Chemistry

question: The most basic hydroxide among the following is
explanation:
In f -block element, due to lanthanide contraction, the size of $\mathrm{M}^{3+}$ ions (i.e. $\mathrm{Ce}^{3+}, \mathrm{Lu}^{3+}, \mathrm{Gd}^{3+}$ ) decreases and thus, basic strength of their hydroxide also decreases. The order of size of given $\mathrm{M}^{3+}$ ions is
$\mathrm{Ce}^{3+}>\mathrm{Gd}^{3+}>\mathrm{Lu}^{3+}$. Hence, $\mathrm{Ce}(\mathrm{OH})_{3}$ is the most basic hydroxide among all of these.
Lanthanide contraction $\propto \dfrac{1}{\text { basic strength }}$.
subject: Chemistry

question: The correct increasing order of basic character of $\mathrm{Ce}(\mathrm{OH})_{3}, \mathrm{Gd}(\mathrm{OH})_{3}$ and $\mathrm{Nd}(\mathrm{OH})_{3}$ is
explanation:

As a result of decrease in size, their covalent character between lanthanoid ion and $\mathrm{OH}^{-}$ion increases from $\mathrm{La}^{3+}$ to $\mathrm{Lu}^{3+}$. Therefore, the basic character of hydroxides decreases from $\mathrm{La}(\mathrm{OH})_{3}$ to $\mathrm{Lu}(\mathrm{OH})_{3}$. Hence, the correct order of basic character will be
$\mathrm{Gd}(\mathrm{OH})_{3} < \mathrm{Nd}(\mathrm{OH})_{3} < \mathrm{Ce}(\mathrm{OH})_{3}$.

question:
Which of the following elements have half-filled f-orbitals in their ground state?
(Given : atomic numbers ${\rm{Sm}} = 62;{\rm{Eu}} = 63;{\rm{Tb}} = 65$
${\rm{Gd}} = 64,{\rm{Pm}} = 61)$
$\begin{array}{lllll}\text { A. } \mathrm{Sm} & \text { B. Eu } & \text { C. } \mathrm{Tb} & \text { D. Gd } & \text { E. Pm }\end{array}$
Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
explanation:
Half-filled configuration of f - orbitals will have 7 electrons.
$({\text{A}})\,\,{\text{Sm}}\,(62)\quad \left[ {{\text{Xe}}} \right]4{{\text{f}}^6}6{{\text{s}}^2}$
$({\text{B}})\,\,{\text{Eu}}\,(63)\quad \,\,\left[ {{\text{Xe}}} \right]4{{\text{f}}^7}6{{\text{s}}^2}$
$({\text{C}})\,\,{\text{Tb}}\,(65)\quad \,\,\left[ {{\text{Xe}}} \right]4{{\text{f}}^9}6{{\text{s}}^2}$
$({\text{D}})\,\,{\text{Gd}}\,(64)\quad \,\left[ {{\text{Xe}}} \right]4{{\text{f}}^7}5{{\text{d}}^1}6{{\text{s}}^2}$
$({\text{E}})\,\,{\text{Pm}}\,(61)\quad \,\left[ {{\text{Xe}}} \right]4{{\text{f}}^5}6{{\text{s}}^2}$
Hence, Europium and Gadolinium have halffilled f-subshell.

question:
Assertion: Most of the trivalent lanthanide ions are coloured both in the solid state and in aqueous solution.

Reason: The elements with ${\rm{xf}}$ electrons have a similar colour to those of ${\rm{(14 - x) f}}$ electrons.
explanation: Lanthanide ions exhibit distinct colors owing to the partially filled f-orbitals within their electronic configurations. This unique characteristic allows for $\mathrm{f}$-f transitions, leading to the absorption of specific wavelengths of light and the manifestation of various colors in these ions. As  f  subshell can have a maximum of 14 electrons,  xf  and (14-x) f, both will have the same number of unpaired electrons for transition .Hence,they both show same color. So option (2) is correct.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.

Assertion is incorrect but Reason is correct.
